meta.name: All-In Post-Training Panorama
meta.description: A living map of LLM post-training methods, systems, and agentic RL.

tracks.title: Alignment Foundations
tracks.summary: Instruction tuning, preference learning, and the baseline vocabulary of post-training.

tracks.title: Reasoning RL
tracks.summary: RLVR, GRPO-family algorithms, and verifiable rewards for math, code, and reasoning.

tracks.title: Capability Fusion
tracks.summary: Specialist training, on-policy distillation, and multi-objective reward integration.

tracks.title: Agentic RL
tracks.summary: Multi-turn environments, tool actions, dense reward, and long-horizon credit assignment.

tracks.title: Systems Infrastructure
tracks.summary: Rollout engines, sandboxes, replay, scheduling, prefix reuse, and production bottlenecks.

tracks.title: Evaluation and Safety
tracks.summary: Benchmarks, reward hacking controls, tool safety, and deployment guardrails.

references.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
On-Policy Distillation of Language Models: Learning from Self-Generated Mistakes
DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
VinePPO: Refining Credit Assignment in RL Training of LLMs
DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
Kimi k1.5: Scaling Reinforcement Learning with LLMs
DAPO: An Open-Source LLM Reinforcement Learning System at Scale
Reinforcing Multi-Turn Reasoning in LLM Agents via Turn-Level Reward Design
Group-in-Group Policy Optimization for LLM Agent Training
MiniMax-M1: Scaling Test-Time Compute Efficiently with Lightning Attention
A Practitioner's Guide to Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning
GDPO: Group reward-Decoupled Normalization Policy Optimization for Multi-reward RL Optimization
Forge: Scalable Agent RL Framework and Algorithm

Teacher-Guided Policy Optimization for On-Policy Reasoning Distillation under Large Policy Divergence

nodes.title: SFT + RLHF
nodes.summary: Instruction demonstrations plus preference-trained reward models made base LMs follow user intent more reliably.
nodes.why: This remains the conceptual root of modern post-training pipelines, even when newer methods replace the reward model or PPO loop.

nodes.title: Direct preference optimization
nodes.summary: DPO turns pairwise preferences into a direct classification-style objective without fitting a separate reward model.
nodes.why: It is a strong baseline for alignment work where online rollouts and reward-model training are unnecessary or too expensive.

nodes.title: RL with verifiable rewards
nodes.summary: Math, code, and tool tasks can provide deterministic success signals, reducing dependence on subjective human labels.
nodes.why: Verifiable rewards are the scaling lever behind reasoning-focused RL and many agentic training recipes.

nodes.title: GRPO and group-relative baselines
nodes.summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization removes the learned critic by comparing multiple sampled answers for the same prompt.
nodes.why: Critic-free optimization lowers memory and stability costs, making it attractive for large-model reasoning RL.

nodes.title: DAPO stability recipe
nodes.summary: DAPO combines decoupled clipping, dynamic sampling, token-level loss, and soft overlong punishment for large-scale RL.
nodes.why: It documents practical fixes for failure modes such as entropy collapse and ineffective all-correct/all-wrong groups.

nodes.title: CISPO and off-policy reuse
nodes.summary: CISPO clips importance-sampling weights and is reported with MiniMax-M1 as a route to more efficient RL updates.
nodes.why: Off-policy reuse and stable gradients are becoming core engineering differentiators for expensive rollout pipelines.

nodes.title: On-policy distillation
nodes.summary: A student samples its own trajectories while a teacher supplies token-level supervision on the student's distribution.
nodes.why: OPD bridges imitation and online training, reducing distribution mismatch compared with fixed offline distillation data.

nodes.title: Specialist RL then fusion
nodes.summary: Train domain specialists independently, then merge capabilities through distillation or mixed reward objectives.
nodes.why: This addresses destructive interference when math, code, chat, safety, and agent objectives are optimized together.

nodes.title: Multi-reward normalization
nodes.summary: GDPO decouples reward normalization so different reward dimensions do not collapse into indistinguishable advantages.
nodes.why: Multi-objective post-training needs reward geometry that preserves disagreement instead of washing it out.

nodes.title: Self-distillation from feedback
nodes.summary: SDPO converts rich textual feedback into dense policy supervision without an external teacher or explicit reward model.
nodes.why: It points toward self-improvement loops that exploit environment feedback beyond scalar pass/fail rewards.

nodes.title: Environment-reward-policy co-design
nodes.summary: Agentic RL must design the environment, reward density, and policy optimizer together instead of treating them as separable.
nodes.why: Long-horizon tasks expose sparse rewards, delayed credit, stateful tools, and exploration collapse.

nodes.title: Turn-level reward design
nodes.summary: Dense intermediate rewards can improve multi-turn agents when their discriminative direction matches the final task objective.
nodes.why: Reward shaping is helpful only when it supports credit assignment instead of rewarding misleading intermediate behavior.

nodes.title: Step-level credit assignment
nodes.summary: GiGPO and StepPO move credit assignment toward repeated states or agent action boundaries instead of raw token positions.
nodes.why: Tool-calling agents make decisions at observation/action boundaries, so token-only objectives can be noisy.

nodes.title: Scalable agent RL systems
nodes.summary: Forge-style systems emphasize arbitrary agent scaffolds, asynchronous scheduling, data pools, and efficiency-aware rewards.
nodes.why: Production post-training is bounded by rollout throughput, environment variance, and sample efficiency as much as by algorithms.

nodes.title: Sandboxed replay and traceability
nodes.summary: Agentic datasets need deterministic environment traces, tool logs, and replay contracts for validation and safety review.
nodes.why: Without reproducible traces, rewards become hard to audit and model improvements become difficult to attribute.

nodes.title: Capability and safety evaluation matrix
nodes.summary: Post-training evaluation should connect reasoning, coding, tool use, long-horizon planning, safety, and reward hacking checks.
nodes.why: A single benchmark cannot represent post-training quality once models interact with external environments.

edges.summary: DPO simplifies preference optimization by removing the explicit reward-model plus PPO loop.
Verifiable domains let post-training scale beyond subjective preference labels.
GRPO is a common critic-free optimizer for verifiable reasoning tasks.
DAPO addresses known large-scale GRPO instability modes.
Both respond to rollout cost and stability pressure with different clipping and reuse strategies.
Domain-specific RL specialists can later be merged into a unified model.
OPD supplies a stable path for merging specialist behaviors.
Multi-reward methods address reward conflicts inside or after specialist fusion.
Self-distillation explores how feedback can replace or augment external teachers.
Single-turn verifiable RL expands into stateful multi-turn environments.
Sparse final rewards often need intermediate signals in long-horizon tasks.
Step-level methods move reward assignment closer to agent decisions.
Large environment pools and asynchronous systems are needed for industrial-scale agent RL.
Traceability and sandboxing keep large rollout systems auditable.
Reproducible traces support benchmark evaluation and safety audits.
